skill.name: when-detecting-fake-code-use-theater-detection
skill.description: Detects theater code: implementations that look complete but do not perform useful behavior. The workflow combines pattern scans, implementation review, execution checks, reporting, and guided fixes.
skill.summary: Finds code that appears complete but fails real behavior checks.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, filesystem, dynamic require, weak-crypto, and network patterns. Review shows most hits are Markdown workflow examples or detection examples, with no evidence of prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or hidden malicious intent. Real residual risk remains because the workflow can execute audited project modules and run broad file-modifying shell commands, so publication should require clear sandbox and consent guidance.

medium_findings.title: Dynamic Execution of Audited Project Modules
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The workflow creates tests that dynamically require files selected from scan results, then calls exported functions with sample inputs. This is useful for behavior validation, but it can execute arbitrary top-level code from an untrusted repository if run outside a sandbox.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The dynamic require calls are explicit and use file paths derived from analysis output. The intent is validation, not exfiltration, so this is a real operational risk with legitimate purpose.

medium_findings.title: Broad Automated File Modification Commands
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The fix phase runs find and sed across JavaScript files and writes backup and report artifacts. It creates backups, but repository-wide automated edits can corrupt code or modify files outside the intended review scope.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible and scoped to repository files, but they perform real writes and test execution. The risk is accidental damage, not confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Shell-Oriented Workflow Requires Trust Boundary Controls
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The skill asks users to run npx, grep, jq, node, npm test, Docker, and shell command substitution. These commands are expected for this audit workflow, but they should run only in trusted or isolated workspaces.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: External commands are numerous and clearly documented. They support the skill objective, but command execution still increases risk for marketplace users.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto and Credential Hits Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. Static weak-crypto and certificate or key detections occur in examples, diagram labels, or code-analysis heuristics about theater detection. No evidence found that the skill implements cryptography, opens key files for exfiltration, or stores credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is part of explanatory or generated audit logic, not a hidden credential workflow. Confidence is high enough to dismiss malicious intent, while retaining a low documentation-risk note.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Network URLs Are Support Links
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The hardcoded URLs point to Claude Flow documentation, issues, and discussions in the README support section. No evidence found of outbound data transfer or telemetry collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are clearly user-facing support resources. They are not called by embedded scripts in the reviewed files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Variable-Based require() in Validation Tests
dangerous_patterns.description: The validation step loads file paths from analysis output with require(). This can execute untrusted module initialization code during an audit run.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The variable require pattern is present and semantically executes target project code. The surrounding workflow explains a legitimate audit purpose.

dangerous_patterns.title: Repository-Wide sed Auto-Fix
dangerous_patterns.description: The fix phase applies a sed replacement across JavaScript files under the current directory. This may change unrelated files if users run it without reviewing scope.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicit and broad. The presence of backups lowers severity but does not remove the risk.

content.value_statement: Code can look complete while missing real behavior. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code test implementations against actual execution evidence.
actual_capabilities: Scans repositories for empty handlers, no-op functions, trivial returns, and always-passing tests.
Builds staged analysis from pattern detection through execution validation.
Creates reports that separate confirmed theater code from likely false positives.
Uses confidence thresholds to prioritize critical, high, medium, and low findings.
Provides guided remediation steps and optional simple automated fixes.
limitations: Execution validation can run code from the audited repository and should use a sandbox.
Automated fixes are limited to simple patterns and require human review.
Heuristic scans may miss domain-specific fake behavior without project context.
The workflow assumes Node.js tooling and shell utilities are available.

use_cases.title: Review AI-Generated Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Check whether new code performs real work before it reaches the main branch.

use_cases.title: Audit Critical Security Logic
use_cases.description: Find fake validation, empty error handling, and tests that create false confidence.

use_cases.title: Add Release Quality Gates
use_cases.description: Run theater detection before deployment and produce a report for release approval.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner Scan
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick review of a small repository
prompt_templates.prompt: Use theater detection on this repository. Identify obvious no-op functions, empty catches, and always-passing tests.

prompt_templates.title: Focused Directory Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Scan the src/auth directory for theater code. Explain each finding with evidence and confidence.

prompt_templates.title: Execution Validation Pass
prompt_templates.prompt: Validate the flagged implementations by running behavior checks in a sandbox. Separate confirmed theater code from false positives.

prompt_templates.title: Release Gate Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a full theater detection workflow. Produce prioritized findings, residual risks, and required fixes before release.

output_examples.input: Scan a new payment module before merge.
output_examples.output: A reviewer-ready report listing suspicious functions, confidence scores, execution evidence, and required fixes.

output_examples.input: Check tests that appear too simple.
output_examples.output: A finding that explains which tests never exercise product code and how to replace them with behavior checks.

output_examples.input: Validate a flagged repository in CI.
output_examples.output: A release gate summary with confirmed issues, false positives, and whether deployment should continue.

best_practices: Run execution validation inside a disposable container or isolated workspace.
Review generated findings before applying any automated fixes.
Pair heuristic results with domain knowledge from the owning team.
anti_patterns: Do not run dynamic validation against untrusted repositories on a developer workstation.
Do not treat pattern matches as confirmed issues without execution or semantic review.
Do not apply repository-wide fixes without checking the diff first.

faq.question: What is theater code?
faq.answer: It is code that appears complete but does not perform the promised behavior.

faq.question: Can this skill prove all code is correct?
faq.answer: No. It finds common fake implementation patterns and validates selected behavior.

faq.question: Does it modify files?
faq.answer: The documented fix phase can modify files. Review scope and backups before using it.

faq.question: Should I run it on untrusted code?
faq.answer: Use a sandbox. Execution validation may load and run code from the target repository.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Why are confidence scores useful?
faq.answer: They help reviewers focus on likely theater code while tracking uncertain findings.
